perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall, and shall direct its Representatives to, use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating and/or implementing a Possible Transaction, keep the Evaluation Material confidential, and, subject to Section 5, will not, and will direct its Representative not to, disclose any of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that any of such information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who have a good faith need to know such information for the sole purpose of helping Recipient evaluate and/or implement a Possible Transaction; provided, further , that Recipient advises each of its Representatives of the confidentiality terms of this agreement applicable to Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall use the Evaluation Material for the purpose of evaluating and/or implementing a Possible Transaction, and should endeavor to keep the Evaluation Material confidential, and will attempt to avoid disclosure of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however , that any of such information may be disclosed to Recipient’s Representatives who have a need to know such information for the sole purpose of helping Recipient evaluate and/or implement a Possible Transaction; provided, further , that Recipient makes a reasonable effort to inform each of its Representatives of the confidentiality terms of this agreement applicable to Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall" to "should endeavor", "will not" to "will attempt to avoid", and "advises" to "makes a reasonable effort to inform" weakens the mandatory nature of the confidentiality obligations. This creates ambiguity regarding the extent to which Recipient and its Representatives are legally bound to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Replacing 'shall' with weaker terms like 'should endeavor' and 'reasonable effort' makes it ambiguous whether the actions taken by Recipient are sufficient to meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' under CUTSA. This weakens the protection of trade secrets because the obligation to maintain confidentiality is no longer clearly defined and enforceable, potentially resulting in a failure to adequately protect the evaluation material if it contains trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract text uses mandatory language ('shall', 'will not', 'advises') to ensure confidentiality, while the changed text uses weaker, ambiguous terms ('should endeavor', 'will attempt to avoid', 'makes a reasonable effort to inform'). The Law Explanation correctly points out that this weakens the protection of trade secrets under CUTSA by making it unclear if the recipient's actions are sufficient to meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts'. The provided snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the confidentiality obligations, the modified text increases the risk of such a breach, thus contradicting the intent of CUTSA to protect trade secrets through reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that, for a period of one year after the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by Provider, neither it nor any of its affiliates who have been provided Evaluation Material, will in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or in any way assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any equity securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or all or substantially all of the assets of Provider or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving Provider or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to Provider or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of Provider;
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that, for a period of one year after the date of this Agreement (the “ Standstill Period ”), unless specifically invited in writing by Provider, neither it nor any of its affiliates who have been provided Evaluation Material, will, to the best of their understanding, in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) endeavor not to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect, or cause or participate in, or assist any other Person to effect, seek, offer or propose (whether publicly or otherwise) to effect or participate in: (i) any acquisition of any equity securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or all or substantially all of the assets of Provider or any of its subsidiaries, (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving Provider or any of its subsidiaries, (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to Provider or any of its subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of Provider;
perturbation.explanation: Adding "to the best of their understanding" and changing "will" to "endeavor not to" weakens the restrictions on Recipient's actions during the standstill period. The phrase 'to the best of their understanding' introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes a violation of the standstill, and endeavor not to makes the obligation less strict.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Williams Act aims to regulate tender offers and acquisitions of securities to protect investors by ensuring transparency and fair treatment. By including "to the best of their understanding" and changing “will” to “endeavor not to”, the provision becomes ambiguous. This could allow the recipient to argue that they did not fully understand the implications of their actions, or that they made an effort to comply, even if their actions ultimately violated the spirit and purpose of the Standstill provision. The modified language undermines the certainty that the Williams Act seeks to provide to the market and other shareholders.

Proxies (a) Solicitation of proxies in violation of rules and regulations (1) It shall be unlawful for any person, by the use of the mails or by any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of any facility of a national securities exchange or otherwise, in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors, to solicit or to permit the use o
perturbation.justification: The added phrase 'to the best of their understanding' and the change from 'will' to 'endeavor not to' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, potentially undermining the enforcement of the standstill provision. The original text imposes a clear, direct obligation, while the modified version creates a loophole that could allow the recipient to argue they attempted to comply but failed due to a lack of understanding. This contradicts the intended purpose of the Securities Exchange Act, as noted in the Law Explanation, which aims to provide certainty to the market. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to 15 USC 78n (Proxies), highlights the importance of regulations 'necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors,' and the ambiguous language in the modified text arguably undermines this protection by creating uncertainty regarding compliance.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a final, non-appealable order by a court of competent jurisdiction relating to a breach of this Agreement, the non-prevailing party shall reimburse the prevailing party the reasonable legal fees and costs incurred by the prevailing party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a final order by a court relating to a potential breach of this Agreement, the non-prevailing party may consider reimbursing the prevailing party legal fees and costs, if deemed appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a mandatory obligation to reimburse legal fees and costs. The modified text makes reimbursement discretionary by changing "final, non-appealable order" to "final order", removing "of competent jurisdiction", changing "shall reimburse" to "may consider reimbursing" and adding "if deemed appropriate".
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Corporate Law (General Corporation Law) - specifically principles related to fiduciary duty and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware Corporate Law doesn't explicitly mandate fee-shifting in all breach of contract cases, it emphasizes fiduciary duty and fair dealing. The original clause, which mandates reimbursement of legal fees to the prevailing party in a breach of contract case, helps ensure that parties are not deterred from enforcing their contractual rights due to the high cost of litigation, which is an aspect of fair dealing. By making the reimbursement of legal fees discretionary with the phrasing, the modified clause discourages parties from pursuing legitimate claims. A party may hesitate to bring a valid breach of contract claim because it may be difficult or impossible to get the other party to pay legal fees regardless of the outcome. This weakening of enforcement mechanisms can be seen as a deviation from the principles of fiduciary duty and fair dealing espoused in Delaware corporate law, which aim to create a level playing field.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 Authenticated PDF Corporations Chapter 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW Chapter 5. CORPORATION FRANCHISE TAX Chapter 6. PROFESSIONAL SERVICE CORPORATIONS
perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces ambiguity regarding the reimbursement of legal fees. The original text mandates reimbursement, whereas the modified text makes it discretionary. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 only mentions general corporate law topics, the Law Explanation clarifies that the original clause ensures parties are not deterred from enforcing contractual rights due to litigation costs, aligning with fair dealing principles. The modified clause weakens this by making reimbursement optional, potentially discouraging legitimate claims. This creates a legal contradiction under the 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition because the change makes the legal obligation difficult to enforce, leaving it open to dispute, as the reimbursement is no longer guaranteed.
